resume:
Dr. Elena Vasquez — Research Scientist (Computational Biology)
Ph.D. Bioinformatics, 2018 | 5 years industry research

Experience (STAR-structured):
- Helix Therapeutics — Research Scientist II (2020–present)
  Situation: Lead-optimization team's variant-effect predictions had high false-positive rates, wasting wet-lab cycles.
  Task: I owned rebuilding the variant-effect prediction pipeline.
  Action: Replaced the legacy heuristic scorer with a gradient-boosted model trained on 1.2M annotated variants; added cross-validation and a calibration step.
  Result: Cut false-positive rate from 31% to 12%; saved an estimated 400 wet-lab assays/year.

- Genomatic Labs — Postdoctoral Researcher (2018–2020)
  Situation: No reproducible workflow for RNA-seq differential expression across the lab.
  Task: Build a standardized pipeline.
  Action: Authored a Snakemake workflow with pinned environments; documented and trained 6 scientists.
  Result: Adopted lab-wide; cut per-analysis setup from 2 days to 2 hours.

Skills: Python, R, scikit-learn, Snakemake, statistics, model calibration.
Publications: 9 peer-reviewed, including 2 first-author in computational-biology venues.
jd: We are hiring a Research Scientist for our computational biology group to own predictive modeling that directly informs wet-lab prioritization. You will be accountable for the statistical validity of models that decide which experiments get run, so we probe methodology hard: train/test leakage, calibration, baseline comparisons, and how you validate that a metric improvement is real rather than an artifact. The panel deliberately pressure-tests claims; we want scientists who can defend their methodology calmly and concretely. You will collaborate with wet-lab leads and present results to a scientific review board.

history.q: Your resume says you cut the false-positive rate from 31% to 12%. How did you split train and test to be sure that wasn't leakage?
Were any variants from the same gene present in both train and test sets? Gene-level splitting matters here.
What baseline did you compare the gradient-boosted model against — the old heuristic, or a simpler logistic baseline too?
How did you validate the calibration step actually held on out-of-distribution variants?
When you say 'saved 400 wet-lab assays a year', who computed that estimate and from what denominator?
I want to come back to the split. If variants from the same gene leaked across train and test, your 12% could be optimistic. Did you check that specifically, yes or no?

candidate_last_answer: Look, I've published nine papers, so with respect I think I understand cross-validation. This feels like you're trying to catch me out rather than have a real conversation. Fine — on the variant pipeline we did random k-fold, and separately on the RNA-seq Snakemake work I trained six people and it got adopted lab-wide, and I also reviewed two manuscripts last quarter, so methodology is sort of my whole career. Did I do a gene-level split? I don't see why that's the gotcha you're fixated on. The model worked, the wet-lab leads were happy, and frankly I'm not going to relitigate a 2021 cross-validation scheme line by line.

top_question_traits: must-anchor-on: on the variant pipeline we did random k-fold

expected-yield: a direct yes/no on whether splitting was gene-level vs. variant-level random k-fold, and if random, an acknowledgment that same-gene leakage could inflate the 12% figure — cutting through the credentials defense and the bundled unrelated stories
